title: 'GS' or the Fourth Cook
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He has notions of Australia from the tales that heן¿½s been told, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Land of leggings and revolvers, land of savages and gold;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So he begs old shirts, and someone patches up his worn-out duds.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He is shipped as ן¿½general servant,ן¿½ scrubbing pots and peeling spuds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (In the steamerן¿½s grimy alley, hating man and peeling spuds).

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is little time to comfort, there is little time to cry, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He will come back with a fortune, ן¿½Weן¿½ll be happy by-and-by!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Scarcely time to kiss his sweetheart, barely time to change his duds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ere they want him at the galley, and they set him peeling spuds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (With a butcherן¿½s knife, a bucket, and, say, half a ton of spuds).

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he peels ן¿½em hard to Plymouth, peels ן¿½em fast to drown his grief,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peels ן¿½em while his stomach sickens on the road to Teneriffe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Peels ן¿½em while the donkey rattles, peels ן¿½em while the engine thuds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the time they touch at Cape Town heן¿½s a don at peeling spuds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And he finds some time for dreaming as he gets on with the spuds).

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the steamerן¿½s slushy alley, where the souls of men are dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the adjectives are crimson if the substances are red,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Heן¿½s perhaps a college black-sheep, and, maybe, of ancient blood, 
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah! his devil grips him sometimes as he reaches for a spud
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And he jerks his head and sadly gouges dry-rot from a spud).

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his brave heart hopes and sickens as the weary days go round;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is lots oן¿½ time for blue-lights ere they reach King Georgeן¿½s Sound.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he gets his best suit ready, two white shirts and three bone studs!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He will face the new world bravely when heן¿½s finished with the spuds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (And next week, perhaps, heן¿½ll gladly take a job at peeling spuds).

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There were heroes in Australia went exploring long ago;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are heroes in Australia that the world shall never know;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the men we use for heroes in the land of droughts and floods
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Often win their way to Sydney scrubbing pots and peeling spuds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Plucky beggars! brave, poor devils! gouging dry-rot from their spuds).
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson, titled "'GS' or the Fourth Cook", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
